questions.answer: Polymers are large molecules composed of many repeated subunits, known as monomers.

questions.q: Give an example of a natural polymer.
questions.answer: Cotton is an example of a natural polymer.
A monomer is a small molecule that can react with other monomers to form a large polymer chain.

questions.q: What is the difference between addition and condensation polymerization?
questions.answer: In addition polymerization, monomers add to the growing chain without the loss of any atoms, whereas in condensation polymerization, monomers combine to form a polymer with the loss of small molecules like water or methanol.

Polymerization can be classified into two main types: addition polymerization and condensation polymerization. Addition polymerization occurs when monomers add to the growing chain without the loss of any atoms. Condensation polymerization occurs when monomers combine to form a polymer with the loss of small molecules like water or methanol.
key_points: Examples of addition and condensation polymerization

questions.q: What is the difference between homopolymer and copolymer?
questions.answer: A homopolymer is a polymer made up of the same type of repeating unit, whereas a copolymer is a polymer made up of two or more different types of repeating units.

questions.q: Give an example of a homopolymer.
questions.answer: Polyethylene is an example of a homopolymer.

questions.q: What is the difference between linear and branched polymers?
questions.answer: Linear polymers have a straight chain structure, whereas branched polymers have a chain structure with side branches. The presence of branches affects the properties of the polymer.

Polymers can have different types of structures, including linear, branched, and cross-linked structures. Linear polymers have a straight chain structure, whereas branched polymers have a chain structure with side branches. Cross-linked polymers have a network structure with covalent bonds between the chains.

questions.q: What is the difference between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers?
questions.answer: Thermoplastic polymers can be melted and reformed multiple times, whereas thermosetting polymers can only be melted and reformed once.

questions.q: Give an example of a thermoplastic polymer.
questions.answer: Polythene is an example of a thermoplastic polymer.

questions.q: What are the applications of polymers?
questions.answer: Polymers have a wide range of applications in various fields, including textiles, plastics, adhesives, elastomers, fibers, and films.

questions.q: Explain the importance of polymers in everyday life.
questions.answer: Polymers play a crucial role in everyday life, from clothing and furniture to medical devices and electronics. They are lightweight, durable, and versatile, making them an essential part of modern life.
key_points: Importance of polymers in everyday life

questions.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of polymers?
questions.answer: The advantages of polymers include their lightweight, durability, and versatility. However, they also have some disadvantages, such as their non-renewable source, non-biodegradable nature, and contribution to pollution.
